Okay, everyone, welcome to the first acceleration call. And I'm excited to be here. And let's see. David, can I hear you? Can you unmute yourself? Yes. Hey, good afternoon. Okay, great. So I've got David on the line. And I think we've got a bunch of people logged in and we're ready to go. So I think we have taken almost 100 questions, taken almost 100 questions in, and we sorted through them and we've got some really good ones that I think highlight the material. And so we're going to answer some of these questions now as we go into the call. And I think, David, are we taking live questions? You want to just go over the format for people so they know what to expect during the call? Sure, I'd be happy to. Yes, absolutely. Hey, everyone, welcome to the first acceleration call in the Pitch Mastery program. Basically, we've curated the questions that you submitted during the registration process for this webinar. So we've selected some of the best questions out of that group. Having said that, though, if you get kind of like something pings in your brain as you're listening to Orrin speak and you want to ask a question here, please do so. Just type into the questions panel on the GoToWebinar interface. I'll see it and then I'll pass it over to Orrin and we can address your question live. Additionally, the basic format for this is that Orrin will call on someone's name. At that point, you would raise your hand. There's a little hand icon on the right hand side of the GoToWebinar control panel. You raise your hand. I will then unmute you and then you will be able to speak directly with Orrin and essentially have a conversation. So if you guys have any questions about that, just go ahead and chat it to me. I think we're all set. Okay, good. I think I want to start off with just a little example or just story just happened last week because this stuff happens to me in real time. I use the material or the method almost every day or a couple of times a week. And so I was on a company call me up to raise a $25 million round for them or they were interested in having it done when their investors call me up and said, hey, will you meet with the company? So I went in to meet with the CEO and the VP of finance. That's a tricky situation because you have the VP of finance trying to protect the CEO from doing anything wrong so they can be very aggressive when they're in the room with this CEO. So as we're discussing the financing, I don't think in terms today of, hey, I'm going to use the moral authority frame. I do it somewhat naturally but I found myself doing it and I thought it would be a good example. So when they said, hey, Orrin, what are your fees like for doing this? Rather than just saying, hey, my fees are 5% or my fees are 7%. I said the following. I said, guys, what I've learned over time from my previous partner, when I was a young guy raising money, I would say, John, I can sell this deal and we can make 10% fees. I can get monster fees on this. And he would say, just calm down. We don't have to be pigs. We can leave some money on the table. We make plenty of money. We don't have to charge 10%. A 10% fee is really above market. Just charge 7%. It's fair. And then I would say, but I can get 10%. He said, I'm proud of you. I know you can get 10% and we can make all that money because the extra 3% can mean another quarter million dollars to each of us. So it's a lot of money. He says, I know you can get it, but let's not be pigs. So I told this to the VP of finance. He would say, hey, listen, in my view, on a deal like this, market is 7%. But I like to leave money on the table for everybody. I told him the experience I had with my old partner. He said, let's not be. I don't have to be a pig. We like to make fair fees and have parity. So he comes back to me and he says, that puts pressure on him because that's the moral authority. We do things in a fair way. And he says, well, in the business that I'm in, we're pigs. We try and get as much as we can. He's trying to show off a little bit. So then I said directly, well, then I wouldn't work with you. If that's true, call me a cab. I would not work with you. We would not get along together. And then he immediately said, of course, I'm joking. I'm joking. So we would never do that. So when you set these frames, like this moral authority frame, it can be very powerful. So this was a couple of days ago in use. The second example is they said, we like you. We want to use you. So the VP of finance says, we want to use you to raise the money. But what we're going to have to do is find two or three firms and have you go present to the board. And then we can choose one because that's a good process. And I said, I'm not going to do that. If you want to do a bake-off, go ahead. But I'm not going to be in it. You've got to choose. You've got to decide you'd like me. I mean, raising money is something very specialized. You're not buying a copier. You've got to deal with a relationship. And if the board is the one making a decision, then I'll go meet with the board. What am I meeting with you guys for? Look, it's your board. You go manage them. I'll decide here with the CEO and the VP of finance who I'm sitting with if we're going to do a deal. But I'm not going to do a bake-off. If that's your process, count me out. I won't do it. So is this a deal we want as a firm? Of course. But we're not going to let ourselves be pushed around. We put the moral authority frame and we prize ourselves. So there's just an example of it happening and working just this week. And then they've come back and said, we want you to do the raise. We want you to do it. I thought we'd open up with that example just to give you a sense that this isn't some material from 10 years ago that I did or read about. This is stuff we use and put in practice really every day. So why don't we now, David, go into the first set of questions and introduce someone to the call. So I think if Scott is there, I might have multiple Scots, but if Scott is there, can you raise your hand? Is that how we pronounce it? So I'll read Scott's question until we can find Scott. If we can't, we'll just address it. Scott, are you there? I'm here. Okay. Hey, buddy. Hi. Yeah, and the most different thing that we do from the competition is that we focus so much on the sign because the usual greeting cards defined in the stores are, yeah, they're very boring and tacky and, yeah, you know. So let me do a couple things with you here. All right, you're going to be on stage with 50 investors. Yeah. And I think unless you prepare ahead of time, you're going to have the pricing problem. Okay. Because you, I can hear in your voice. You view those investors as having the ability to give you what you need, right? You want money because you care. I can hear in your voice. You're so passionate about this. You need them to give you the money. Okay? Yeah. Because you need their approval. You're going to get off stage and you're going to want and need them to accept what you're saying, to approve of you, to ask for another meeting, okay, and to give you the money. And if they don't, it's going to hurt, right? Yeah. It's going to hurt like a bitch. Yeah. You can hear in your voice. So we got to cure that. All right? You got to cure it. First of all, you got to find before you get up on that stage, you got to get somebody else in your pipeline beside those guys. You got to work hard to talk to an uncle, a brother, a sister, somebody you know. It doesn't have to be a lot of money. It could be $250. Just find somebody else, okay? Sure. You got to get rid of that neediness that you're going to have. You're a young guy and it's going to be there naturally. But don't worry. There's 30, 40, 45, 50-year-old guys on this call and gals who would have the same feeling. Me included. You got to understand. I have to do the same work. Yeah. You got to get somebody to say they'll give you money or to give you money, even if it's a small number. Just to cure that feeling. Okay? Yeah. Because now you don't know what it's like for somebody to write you a check. No. No. Dad or whoever to give you some kind of check. Just get a check in your hand. It's going to get rid of some of it. Just one thing. We got a millionaire mentor actually here that's prepared to help us with a lot of things. So he's putting us in contact with the in deal flows with different investors and angels. Great. So organize some alternatives. Those guys cannot be your only alternative. All right? Okay. Yeah. So focus here on pricing. Number two, you got to figure out how to make this fun. You cannot for you. Not for them. It's got to be fun for you. You got to figure out how to get up on that stage and it benefits you. Yeah. Okay? So maybe you've got a great joke that you've always wanted to tell in front of the audience. This has to benefit you. All right? Okay. That's a thing. Right? You have to have some alternatives number one that are real. It could be great if you could get a small check just so you know that you're not reliant on these guys you're going out to. Right? Yeah. Yeah. Absolutely. Okay? You have got to make this fun for you. If you're about to get up on that stage and you're trepidatious and you think you have to somehow impress these guys then our preparation didn't go well. We have to re-prepare. This has got to be fun and beneficial for you. I agree. Okay? Yeah. Right? The third thing, you have to understand these investors. Okay? All they have is money and that is a commodity. Okay? Yes. Right? What you have is more valuable than what they have. What you have can only be gotten in one place and that's from you. All right? What they have can be gotten from anywhere. You already know that. You've got tons of introductions. Okay? You call me up. I'll give you an introduction. Yeah. Okay? What you have is more valuable than what they have. That's what you have sucks but it sounds like it's pretty good. Okay? Yeah. All right? So those are three things that I would like you to really internalize. So you should have fun with it. You've got to recognize all they have is money. They need good deals. The money cannot do anything without a good deal. All right? So I don't want to get you into the mechanics of the pitch. Exactly what to say. Yeah? But for you, your challenge is number one, get some alternatives. Number two, figure out how it's going to be fun for you. And number three, you've got to recognize that what they have is a commodity and what you have is especially important. All right? So I think those are the three things that I want you to nail down. Okay, great. All right, Scott, I'm going to unmute you right now. Hey, Lauren. Hey, Scott. Hey, thanks for joining. So this is a very good question that you've asked here. Let me just put a prelude to it, and then I want to turn it over to you. All right? So Scott is wondering, and rightly so, about being aggressive. Okay? As I said, kind of in the live performance, it's very easy for me to be aggressive with you guys. I'm not selling you anything. Okay? It's very easy for me to say these things in this way. I say it with this tonality so you can understand the structure. All right? There's times to be aggressive, and there's times you can't be. I mean, after all, these are customers. To some degree, they have to like you. You're not likable. Right? So we don't want to kind of copy my tone. I'm showing you this tone so you know you can go there. You can get aggressive. It's okay. Right? But also, it helps you see the structure. It helps you see the structure so you can kind of see what I'm doing in high relief. So Scott, let's get this questioning from you and your challenges around aggression. Tell us what you do. Give us quick introduction to what you do, your product or service, and then what the challenge is. Okay, great. Yeah, I basically sell mobile marketing, consulting, and services. So a lot of my sales are going to be with smaller businesses, one-on-one sales pitches, basically. And what I guess I'm wondering is, I can kind of understand in a high deal environment, you've got maybe you're in front of a board that a lot of the pricing, the framing can really position you. But I guess I'm wondering, in a one-on-one environment, when you're dealing with maybe a small business owner, how can I use that aggressiveness? Or how do I stay away from really turning them off and just saying, oh, you're another sales guy. I don't need you. See you. Yeah. Yeah. So Scott, hearing your voice and kind of getting a sense of where you are in your process, I think what's going to work for you is pricing and building your celebrity status. Now not to misconfuse that and say, hey, I'm a championship swimmer, or I was in college, won a medal, not that kind of celebrity, but celebrity from really knowing your business and how the market is changing and how these changes can affect everybody. So it's like rising above a little bit. So you might say, in this business, there's three, and I've said this over and over, there's three major things changing this market. If you don't understand these, you can really have a disadvantage. You can miss out. You're not really selling. You say, and what's good is I'm glad we're working together because I'm in a different part of the market than you. You deal directly with the customers, but I deal with the carriers or whatever. So I'm glad I'm able to share with you how this market is changing what the window of opportunity is. And if that is real and meaningful. So sort of an education role, kind of go in there, kind of helping to educate them? Yes, except it's not really education. I think that's the wrong word for it. Let me tell you what's going on so you can understand the market window that I operate in. And that people who are going with me through this window are going to have advantages. And people who stay behind and don't know about these forces are going to have disadvantages. That's not purely educational. That is really creating a high contrast decision matrix. You want the red pill, you want the blue pill. And I'm okay with either one because I'm okay. I'm taking advantage of where the market is going and I'm going there and other people are going there with me. So maybe we can get specific and I don't want to chew up too much of everyone's time, but what are the three forces that are changing your market? Well, email marketing is not as effective as it used to be. Now it's texting and the fact that everybody now is searching and you're browsing the internet on a mobile phone versus on a computer. So the market is changing now. If you don't have a mobile optimized website, you're going to miss out on people that are searching on their phone. And give me a third one. A third one is the third product that I sell, which is mobile apps. So custom mobile apps for businesses are now becoming more affordable that the average business now can have their own custom app for their business to help their branding and so on. Okay. So look, I might approach it like this. Gin, from where I sit, I get a different view of the market than you do. It's not better. It's not worse. It's just different. And I want to share with you the lens, the perspective that I get to see the market. Now one, you may be experiencing this on some level, but there's a major change. An email is not working. And you may still be getting some results with it. Maybe you're feeling it's not working. But I can tell you, broad spectrum, that's a dead dinosaur. The people who rely on it and continue to use it are going to see declining rates and it's going to get ugly. That's going to be an expensive black hole. The other thing that's changing is text is on the rise. So mobile text platforms are getting better and better and they're cost efficient. But there's many, many, many choices. You've got to have to understand which ones are working, which ones aren't. And the third is custom mobile apps are absolutely where people are having success. And that's where people are putting emphasis. Now these three things are driving the window of opportunity. These markets change all the time. The current window is driven by email, the lack of success with email, the success of text, and how to use the custom mobile apps. And that's where I am. What I'd like to do is with the short time we have today, is tell you what I'm doing in that market and how we might be able to do something together to take a benefit of these changes. Now that really sets you up to prize. Now your next prize thing is you're going to say, I'm fortunate that I was able to make some time in my schedule and spend some time with you to share some of this insight. I know this stuff and you don't. You know things I don't. And that's fine, but I know this stuff and you don't. So we've got to maximize our time together so I can share with you what I have. And then you can go into, then you can go into, look, here's the big idea. And you can start talking about your product. Then you're going to go into the big idea. We'll do a big idea with someone at some point. And then the problem and the solution and how it works. And now you're into your story or your pitch. For you, I think that's your point of entry. Just hearing your voice and hearing what your challenge is, it's a quick recap. For the situation here is you've got to elevate your status. You've got to do that through some celebrity. The more you compile on celebrity stuff, companies are asking you, companies much larger than you ask me to do this for them. And so it begs the question, well, why would you be working with this small little local company if you're working with big companies? And then you're going to fill that gap by saying something like, I, this is great. I recognize you're small. And if we work together, you would be a small account for me. That's the fact. But I do this because it's how I meet new companies and new people and how a lot of people get to hear about it. But I don't take on a lot of small customers. I do some that I like. And if I really think they're going to let me profit and if I really think they're going to be a good customer and they're going to be fun to work with and they understand what's happening so I don't have to hold their hand, then I will work with someone like you. You just continue to pile it on. Because it's true. These are not mech. Do these things resonate with you as accurate? Yeah, they do. Because I think the celebrity thing I get. I have a book that I've written on online marketing and that I can kind of use as part of that. But I just need to go in there with more pricing. Yeah, you want to be careful about, I wrote a book, right? I wrote a book. And I never lead with it. It can be awkward. So you want to kind of almost let them discover it. So for me, I'll tell you something that's interesting. And for everyone, next week I'm going out to New York. The third largest ad agency in the world has asked me to come in and spend some time with their CEO to show them some pitching. So normally, I try to just stick to my knitting. We did this product that helped companies raise money. I don't do a lot of coaching. But it's an eight billion dollar company. And they're an ad agency. They're a counter-copper tone. GM, Visa, Cadillac. So when a company like that invites me in to teach them pitching, it's very exciting for me. It's very validating. And so they found me from the book. It's one of the chief marketing officer of this large ad agency was reading the book and the chief marketing officer of another ad agency called them up and said, hey, you have to read this book. Pitch anything. It's awesome. And Chris goes, I'm already reading it. Let's talk to this guy. So that's how I got involved with him from the book. So you see what I did there? I'm not hitting you over the head with I have a book. I'm just kind of weaving it in truthfully in a way that's relevant. You can't just pull the book out and go, I wrote a book. OK. I think a lot of people have written some content. So hopefully we can hear this idea brought up several times. Like I wrote some content or a PDF or a book. I know it's not enough time for you to really get into it. And then shoot your questions, and then we'll try and tackle. OK. I had a couple of questions, but you mentioned the one on humor. Basically, what we do, I have a software company, and we help dispatch centers improve dispatch operations in the public sector. Oh, yeah, I remember. That's right. Yeah. So in overall, I mean, humor in timing is a great thing when delivered correctly. But I'm also looking for scalability, Aaron. I mean, I've got guys that can deliver that stuff cold and other ones that can't. But really, in terms of using humor effectively, almost like in a Seinfeld situation as opposed to telling a joke and having it bomb or offend someone. So I was kind of curious on how to deliver it effectively. One, so it works. And two, so it's great to be confident, not so great to be cocky, the win deal. That's right. That's right. So humor can go wrong. So comedians, when you talk to comedians, one thing they'll say is you can't teach timing, right? And humor is all about timing. And so let me go through my thoughts on it. One is you cannot have jokes. OK. Got you. You cannot have any prepackaged jokes at all, right? Those will always be bad. Any time that you have for someone to listen to a joke, that means you've got really, really good attention from them, you want to use that attention to pitch your product. So I want to make the point here. Not sure I have a graphic on it. I'll just cycle through my graphics quickly. So, let's just leave it like this. You are fighting constantly for attention. OK. Attention equals convincing power. I've said this before, if you could sit me down, tie me to a chair, take my eyes open, OK, and make me listen attentive for two hours, you're going to sell me what you have. I'm going to buy it. Because if you can make me to pay attention, you are going to do a good job of convincing. The issue is time. So attention, if attention equals convincing power, you want to take advantage of all the attention you can get to really give proof points around your product and talk about the big idea, the problem, the solution, how it works, the value proposition, and the economics and the time constraint. You want to spend any attention so you don't want to be telling jokes with that time. It's number one. Number two, it is important for your guys to have fun. So I think without getting enough specifics, unless you have an example of, hey, is this funny or how could we make this funny, probably the first step before telling your guys to scale up and say, hey, use humor because that requires timing. I think the first step, Joe, is to make sure they have a way to have fun. And then the humor will come naturally out of that. Got you. Okay. Sit down with your guys and say, this has got to be, this is our life. We got to have fun with our life. This has got to be fun for us. And the customer or the buyer can have fun along with us. We're not there to entertain them. We're there to provide value. We're there to have a good time ourselves. Is that a step you think you could get to, a way that your guys or gals can have fun in the meetings? Oh, no, I think we can. And one thing, because again, on our average purchase order size, you're talking 25K. So much smaller deals and a lot of this stuff happens remotely in a similar meeting like this. Obviously, humor and timing is really critical because of the fact that you lose body language and tonality and everything else because it's pretty much, the visual component is not there. So I definitely think we can get our people to have fun on deals. Okay. So then you can have, if you've got two guys, or a girl and a guy that work together and they have a good banter, it's okay. Which is great is for them to be having fun by themselves. And then that will bring the customer in. So David and I have a lot. By the way, David, another Heineken if I can get one because these guys are making me nervous and it settles me down. So if we can get another one brought in, and also I'm running low on nachos. I'm brewing the Heineken's right now. So, Joe, we can have fun together, right? And it's going to bring people in and they're going to want to contribute. So let me tell you where this goes within the process. This is again setting the frame, right? Where we are very social on our side, right? We know how to use humor amongst ourselves. We are self-deprecating. We're pretty cool guys to get along with. Yes, we're selling this product, but in terms of setting the frame, right? We're good people to work with. And then you might have some dialogue like, oh, this customer reminds me of the guy who said he didn't need it. But then he orders 50 of them and shuts down the factory. You got to be careful with guys like this, right? They're sleepers. Still waters run deep. So that's stuff that you can work on. I think that one's pretty good. I don't know your business well enough, right? And that's just off the top of my head. And maybe I can give you another one. You could push back on the customer a little bit and say, hey, look, we... I'm just trying to think. So we have to make sure, right? When you order it, you use it because we want to use our customers as testimonial. You're not one of those guys that orders five, right? Pays, pre-pays, never uses customer service, right? And it's just 100% gross margin for us. That sounds good, right? But that's not the kind of business we want customers that are fun to work with and they call us up and we grow together. We want you to have problems so we can fix them. What kind of customer are you? The kind of guy just gives us 100% gross margin, right? Puts it in the closet. Or you're somebody we're going to be working with, right? And have some engagement. So I don't know your business well enough and that's just off the top of my head. But I think those are two things that are fun. No, that's great. Now I'll tell you something. Honestly, the customers we've had issues with because we provide 24, 7, 365, you know, around the clock support. So the ones that do have issues, I mean, we build a hell of a bond with them and their repeat business is actually better than customers that don't have any problems and we talk to them once a year and renew the maintenance. So let's abstract that, right? So Robert, I don't know about you. You sound a little too nice, okay? And I'm worried that you're not going to call our 24, 7 customer service, right? Those guys are just going to be sitting around doing nothing. What we know is if a customer will call us, if a customer calls with a problem right away, then we get a bond with that customer. We become friends and the night crew knows them and the day crew knows them, right? And we really become, they become a great customer that lets us profit. So I'm worried that you're too shy and you're going to try and fix everything on your own. I'd love to hear from you. What kind of customer are you? Yeah, that's great. Okay? All right, buddy. I think if you could work on that, then we'll circle back on the next one. But that is, you know, that's somewhere in setting the frame and pricing. So this is all, and this is good, all these questions, but it shows me that we're all kind of thinking about frame setting, pricing, establishing ourselves, getting social, and moving out of the kind of old pattern of doing things, which is so stiff and, you know, doesn't do anything to raise our own status. But the social stuff is great for raising your status because everybody wants to be with a fun, cool, engaging, smart business guy. That's the frame setting that you're doing. So, okay, Joe, I really appreciate it. That's a good question. Excellent. Okay. I appreciate it. Yeah, no, I appreciate you. I do. So I think what would be good is Shane. I don't hear him. Or would you like me to read his question? Yeah, let's do it. He says, greetings. I have a two-part question. One, how do we penetrate a new market? And two, how do we circulate the pitch debt? Yeah, yeah. How do we penetrate a new market? So, well, I'm not, I mean, I'm not an economist. So, I have no idea what to do with that. You penetrate a new market by having lots of cash on hand, a great, amazing product, killer marketing, a great understanding of technology, really helping add value to people's lives, and having a viral effect to users of your product. So, that's how you penetrate a new market. Right. It's important to realize that if you ask vague questions, you're going to get, you know, the answers you're given. I hope that answer wasn't vague. I mean, that should be all easy to do stuff. Please remember to ask very concrete questions. What about the second question? Well, how do we circulate the pitch debt? Yeah. Okay. So, I'm going to go back to an example. I was working with a company raising for a venture fund. Right. So, we really needed limited partners in the fund. A limited partner of venture fund is the guy who backs the fund. So, if you hear, you know, XYZ venture capital, it's typically not their money. The venture capitalists are money managers. They have gone out to very high net worth individuals for the bucket of money they use. The venture capitalist uses to invest with. So, I was helping them raise money. And so, we were targeting guys mainly whose net worth was 200 million to a billion. And one of the guys that was really perfect for the fund is a guy named Nathan Mirwald. He's one of the founders of Microsoft and he's definitely, you know, he's an easily a billionaire. He's a pretty famous guy. You may know him. He wrote this cookbook that's really about the chemistry of cooking when the cookbook of the year award is kind of thing. So, I have his email address and, you know, want him to look at the pitch deck. But for most people, if you send them an email with attachments, it's unsolicited. It's too heavy. It's too heavy. They, especially guys who are very high profile. So, the way I solve this is to send an email out that says, hey Nathan, we have a venture group. I'd like you to look at, I think it's a good fit. If I send you the pitch deck, will you spend five minutes with it? All right. And then just my name, no grand signature. Not, you know, you don't want to have much information or any information below the question mark. So, about half an hour later, I get an email back, yes. You know, then you send the pitch deck along and you can open a dialogue. But that's the right way to do it. If you find somebody and you say, hey, we think we're a good fit together. We got to you through our mutual whatever. Okay. I'd like to send you our pitch deck. It's pretty interesting. Okay. Not your usual stuff. If I send it along, we spend five minutes with it. I think you're going to have a very high success rate circulating your pitch with that. You know, with that approach. Okay. That's great. Hey, Orn, I just heard from Rinaldo. He says, I'm going to have a very specific example. Would you like to have him come on? Yeah, yeah, please. Okay. Great. Rinaldo, I'm going to unmute you right now. Okay. You're with us. Okay, buddy. I'm charging by the minute. Yeah, I can't hear you. Okay. All right. I'm just going to do. Okay. Are there any other questions that we'd like to go through? Marie asks a very good question. You know, there's a whole new. Okay, so we run an auto garage. Can we use it? Yeah, definitely, definitely. How do we want to buy it? How do we make money with it? Okay, so what you can do is, obviously, it basically takes all your information, puts it on the mobile app, on the mobile phone for your customer. You can contact them through push messages to say, hey, I've got a special today only, which works like a text message, but because it's done through the push networks, you're using the big boys of Google and Apple to push it through for you, which is cheaper technology. It's more cost-effective, and it's actually more effective, I suppose. So, yes, so we do about 100 customers a month, right? And we, for about $100,000 of revenue. Okay, what's your guess that we can move the needle with this product? Well, I'll say that's my problem. I don't know. So, there's, so, all right, so I think you, the issue that you're having, I don't think about track record, okay? It's about value proposition. Okay. So what I'd like to do is we're going to circulate exactly where in the tell the story, video module, we go deep into building and delivering the value proposition. That's what you need, okay? Okay. So you need to respond to that for customers like yourself who run one to $3 million businesses who are challenged by expanding marketing, because you have fixed costs. So the more you can bring in, the better net margin you have. So for companies like you that have fixed costs and need to do a better job of marketing, my mobile marketing solution offers the following benefits. One, it's easy to use, easy to install, it's less expensive than what you're doing now, and it can get another 30 to 40% customers. Unlike magazine or internet or radio or newsprint advertising, this stuff is personal, it's automated, and it's cost efficient. All right? What we find is that companies using this product take about a week to install it, about two weeks to start signing their first customers, and within 90 days, they've got a 20 to 25% bump in customer traffic and revenue. All right? That's a, you know, I don't know your product well enough, right? But that's a clear, pretty clear value proposition statement. Now there's going to be a bunch of questions like, but you know, about it, but at least you've nailed down the economics. Does that feel doable? Yeah, definitely, definitely. That just sounded perfect, like how I'm actually supposed to say it. But like for the like the 20% or 30% returns and things like that, I mean, you're obviously needing a trade record to get those figures, or are you just plucking them out of thin air, aren't you, really? Well, I'm plucking them out of thin air, right. That's what I mean. Like that is what you need to say. You need to be given them concrete figures, but then you can't get the figures unless you've got the figures. You know what I mean? Right. But I'd like to introduce you to the Nats eyebrow. Okay. So what you have to do is you have to go down to the Nats eyebrow level. So instead of, so if I say, for example, pitch anything, okay, or pitch mastery can make a 20 to 30% difference in your sales. I think that's probably true. But here's what I know. When you get right down to decision making moment for the buyer, all right, and you do one of the following three things. You seem needy. You seem too similar to other things that the buyer have seen. Yeah. Or what you have is too vague, right? That reduces your chance of selling. Anybody would agree by half. You seem needy. You seem vague. You seem similar. Now I fix those three things for the people I work with. All right. So not when you fix those three things, not only do you not lose a sale, your chances of getting it are a lot better. And on balance, in my experience, on balance, when we fix those things, sales go up. So you see what I did there is I go down to real specifics and that then the customer can decide if those numbers are accurate. But you have to frame it very specifically. I see David yelling at me that I'm spending too much time helping you out. But I care about it. So let's, you practice that. We'll circle back around to you in the next call or offline or in the product if you post questions I'll answer it. I think you need to get your value proposition nailed. You have to be willing to blow out of a deal. You have to go in not needing it, but that's different. You have to be willing to walk away even when you need the sale. So if someone's frame is so strong that it seems unbreakable, and they want to be so out of phase, and I don't know the exact situation, but if they want to be out of phase with your pitch and your presentation, and the way you have running the process, they're not going to be a good customer. Right? So then the way I think, the way I think you do it is you say, actually, I was having a lot of fun, but it's getting kind of awkward because it seems like you care more about the specifics which are readily available than spending the time we have here to try to figure out if we'd like each other. And now I'm getting nervous about you. And I'm getting nervous about you because I want customers that are going to let me profit because we do a great job. We deliver value. And so I'm worried that we wouldn't get along together because this seems out of phase, or this seems very aggressive. And particularly what we teach requires people to get along. Do you think we get along together? Have you bought this kind of product before, an information product or an information service? And how did that work out? So if you ran up against yourself, you would basically walk away. Well no, because I'm fun, right? Because I'm not a pig. I'm not a pig. I do fair deals. And I push people around, but I don't push them out of their process. Right. And at the end, when people do deals with me, they never go, God dang it, how did I get talked into that? They go, I love this relationship that I have. Okay, so that's why I built that, because I can't close everyone. So, but what I do, and I think this is the point, is I have to build in self-respect and the lack of neediness for myself and my team. If they see me roll over, right, or me have a weak frame, or let somebody else push me around, you know, then it ruins their confidence. You have to obey the process. You have to do what I call Gulliver's Travels. And every step of the process, you have to try and do something a little bit better. You have to fill out the form. You have to find a way to put some personality or some charisma or some intriguing something with the form. Okay? So maybe it can go in your pitch. Okay, thanks, Jackson. Okay, David, talk to me. Hey, Aran. Thanks, Jackson, so much. Aran, we've got a really great question from Mark who says he loves, loves, loves this course. And it's a very specific question. I need to read it, though. Do you mind if I read you the setup and then the question? Well, first, can you tell me how Mark feels about the course? He says, I love, love, love it. But it's not clear. Yeah, go for it. Okay, so here's the question. He says, I work for a high-end custom pool builder, small company working with big contractors and high-end resorts most of the time. We build an exceptional product in about half the time of typical pool builders in virtually a hassle-free process. The other value we offer is long-term. Our pools don't have problems over the long-term, 15, 20 years, blah, blah, blah. Our buyers know this, but they are constantly buying with very heavy emphasis on price, regardless of reputation of the builder versus us. And this question is, pull up here. He says, what frame are we confronting and what frame should we use to overcome it? I believe they see us as a prize, but 5% to 15% too expensive. Yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah. Okay, here's clearly, to me, I bet, and I would need to hear your whole pitch, but this sounds like a good one that may be on a following call. David, we could hear the pitch. All right? Right, he had to leave the call, but he said he loves it and wants to hear the answer and be back. My sense of what's going on here is that they believe their status is high, but it's not high enough. Status should be enough to cover that 10% or 15% price difference. So they need to build a narrative around how the market is changing, what they know about the change in the market, the forces that are changing the market, and how a decision today outside of those drivers are going to have severe consequences. So let me try and make that so I can get this in my mind. I don't know this industry well, but the regulations for building are changing. The cost of having plans that are not approved and 60% of plans today that look like they should go through are getting not approved. If you don't understand what's happening in these legislative changes at the county and federal level, you can really get held up for months. So there's regulatory changes. Another thing is the technology is just getting better in the material science. So if you don't understand how this technology is changing, you're buying the last generation. If you're buying the last generation, you're, and that's in starting three years behind from the day it goes in the ground. And the third thing is something else. And then they've got to make these really deeply felt and understood. So they've got to build themselves up as the expert understanding how the forces are changing the market, what it is they do to be in line with the forces, and if you don't kind of step through the new market window, you're really going to be behind on day one that the pool is built. I don't have the value of him on the phone here to really do it back and forth. But clearly what's happening is they haven't built enough insight into why their method and their way of doing it and their company is a real value proposition. And if you don't buy from them, they need to be framed. If you don't buy from them, the day you fill your pool with water the first time, your already three years behind. I think that also should have a broad application for people, but clearly they haven't set the frame strongly enough that they're the experts and they're doing things with a better value proposition. Right. So if someone's focusing on price like that, would you consider that the analyst frame or what kind of frame specifically would that be? So price is an issue always, right? But I think what's happening, if somebody is focusing on price before you're at the end towards the end of the sale, if they're needlessly focusing on price, what's happened is you don't have a compelling enough story. We need to go back to the tell the story module. It's not compelling. And so they are trying to, 10 or 15% difference in price is not enough to make a decision difference. You should be able to sell someone a 10% difference in price. So the narrative and the framing and the intrigue and you as the expert and your status, all of that hasn't been done well enough to justify the difference in price. You can sell across a 10 or 15% difference in price, especially with something big like that that's very... Okay. So let's talk about what tools he has. Doing a poll is very high consequences. You get that wrong. That stuff happens. Luggage, right? I don't know. I've never been in a luggage deal, but it just occurs to me, right? You buy a bad piece of luggage and it blows up halfway between here and Europe and all your stuff falls out. That's very high consequences. So people don't go, I'm going to buy the crappy luggage for 10% savings, right? So this is a matter of really focusing on the stakes, making this a higher stakes decision. I don't really want to say create fear, right? Because that can go in all kinds of negative directions, but you've got to raise the stakes of this decision to where it's more difficult to make...to choose a cheaper product for 10% and potentially have very severe consequences. So I would summarize as I'm talking my way through this, you know, without the benefit of knowing the business, I would summarize it as they haven't made the stakes high enough. Okay, great. We've got just a few minutes left and we have one more question that directly relates to module one. It's from Jimbo, but he doesn't have audio. Would you like me to read the question? Can he use sign language? He can use sign language and then we can kind of like guess it. Perfect. Or Morse code. So opening a Heineken will be one tap, right? And opening a Coca-Cola will be two taps. And if he opens like 60 bottles, yeah. He's texting me right now and he's saying he's sending a messenger pigeon to us. Beautiful. Oh, like in Game of Thrones? So now an army is going to come attack me. All right. Okay. His question is, and this is the last question. Could you do one or two examples of using defiance to get frame control and not blow someone out or piss them off or come off as condescending, rude, etc.? Yeah. Let's see. Wow. I can give, I guess I'll have to give examples from my own experience, right? So I let off the call with an example of a meeting this week where they said, hey, we're interested in using you, Orrin, and your guys to help us raise $25 million. But what you're going to have to do is you and two or three other firms are going to have to present to the board, right? And then we'll choose, and we'll choose one of you guys based on your presentation to the board, right? So defiance without getting blown out is to just say, no, I won't do that. No, I won't do that. That's not a process I'm going to put myself in. It's not good for me. It's good for you. It's not good for me, but it's not good for me, right? And what I am willing to do is demonstrate to you now the value that we deliver, and you guys can make a decision on that. If you decide that it's more important for you guys to have a bake off than choosing a great guy, all right, than having a great guy who's fun to work with, who has a great track record with a good team, all right? If you can't decide, then that's okay. Go through your process, because I know the guys that you're going to put in that process. We all work in the same market, all right? I know those guys. They're good guys, and they might get it done. They're definitely not fun. They definitely don't crawl a mile on glass when it gets hard. When deals start to be difficult, they quit. I've done 35 deals and completed every single one of them. One deal took me six months to raise the money. It was very painful. We lost money, but we don't give up. So I know the guys you're going to do a bake off with, right? And they're not my peer group. So you guys got to decide. You want to work with the best. You can single source me. If you want to bake off, you get to work with those guys. And that's the decision you have, but I'm not going to go in your process. And I think that works. And you don't have to say it aggressively. I can say it again in a different tone of voice. You know, hey guys, I respect that you want to go into a process and everything, but I'm not going to do that. I'm not going to go in your process. So I know the guys, because we all work in the same industry. We're peers. And I know the other guys are going to put in the process, and they just have a totally different business sensibility than we have. When we take on a deal, we get it done no matter what. They take on a deal, and they do best effort. I'm not going to introduce myself to being judged along with that peer group. We do something very specific. And if you want what we have, we're open to discussing with you. If you want to do a bake off, go use those guys. And I think that's a decision you have to make. I can't make it for you. I can help you. I can help you understand my value proposition and know exactly what we can and can't do, but I can't help you make that decision. That's for you to decide. So you can say that in a different tone of voice. You don't have to say it aggressively. So, okay David, I think we got that one nailed down. That's great. Okay. Okay. Guys, we're at time. So if you have any other further questions, please email us at support at pitchanything.com. And we will get to them. I know that there are a few more that came in, kind of at the tail end of the chat there. But we're out of time. We've got to go do some eye banking horsepower. So just a really quick reminder about next week, we're going to have another acceleration call. And it's important to, I think, realize that, you know, Oren is social and we really like live interaction. So come to the webinar with your question prepared and you'll be able to get it answered specifically by Oren. Oren, do you have anything that you'd like to add about the next modules or anything before we sign off? Well I think the, go through the modules now. I want to get people through S and definitely into T, tell the story. I mean that's the pitch. So if you haven't gone all the way through, tell the story, you've got to do that. I want to see some questions starting to come through on the specifics of pitching. So I want these acceleration calls to be in phase with how we're releasing the material. Okay, great. Okay, great.
